1: qĕrā' bĕgārôn — cry aloud, literally cry in the throat. The noun gārôn (גָּרוֹן, throat/neck) rather than simply qôl (voice) — this is visceral, full-bodied proclamation. kaššôpār hārēm qôlĕkā — like a trumpet lift your voice — the shofar is the instrument of covenantal summons, blown on holy days and at times of national reckoning. The content of the proclamation: haggēd lĕ'ammî piš'ām — declare to my people their transgression.
2: wĕ'ôtî yôm yôm yidrōšûn — yet they seek me day after day. The distributive yôm yôm (day by day) signals habit and regularity. wĕda'at dĕrāk̠ay yaḥpāṣûn — and delight to know my ways — the verb ḥāpēṣ (to delight in, desire) is the same root that appears in Isaiah 58:3 for the people's self-serving pleasure; YHWH uses their own word back at them. k̠ĕgôy 'ăšer-ṣĕdāqāh 'āśāh — as if they were a nation doing righteousness — the comparison exposes the performance-gap between appearance and reality.
3: lāmmāh ṣamnû wĕlō'-rā'îtā — Why have we fasted, and you do not see?. The complaint (Qal perfect ṣamnû — we fasted) meets the divine counter: hēn bĕyôm ṣomĕk̠em timṣĕ'û-ḥēpeṣ — behold, on your fast day you find your own pleasure. The noun ḥēpeṣ (חֵפֶץ — delight, desire, business) is the same word as Isaiah 58:2 for knowing YHWH's ways; in fasting they pursue their own ḥēpeṣ, not his. wĕk̠ol-'aṣṣubbêkem tinhagû — and all your workers you drive hard — the Qal imperfect tinhagû (from נָהַג, to drive/drive cattle) applies cattle-driving language to human laborers on fast days.
4: hēn lĕrîb̠ ûlĕmaṣṣāh taṣûmû — behold, you fast for quarreling and strife. The nouns rîb̠ (quarrel, lawsuit) and maṣṣāh (contention, strife) expose the social fabric of their fasting: it accompanies conflict rather than penitence. wĕlĕhakkôt bĕ'egrōp̄ rèša' — to strike with a wicked fist — physical violence on fast days. The conditional: lō'-taṣûmû kayyôm — you will not fast as you do today — implying that such fasting is not real fasting at all.
5: hăk̠āzeh yihyeh ṣôm 'ebĕḥārehu — Is this the fast I choose?. The rhetorical question (expecting a negative answer) defines what the false fast looks like: lak̠pôp̄ k̠'agmōn rō'šô — to bow one's head like a reed — the bulrush ('agmōn, אַגְמוֹן) bends in the wind and springs back; the posture is mechanical, not sincere. wĕśaq wā'ēper yāṣîa' — to spread sackcloth and ashes beneath oneself — the outward signs of mourning without inner transformation. YHWH's indictment: hălāzeh tiqrā' ṣôm — will you call this a fast?
6: hălō' zeh ṣôm 'ebĕḥārehu — Is this not the fast I choose?. The positive counterpart to Isaiah 58:5's rhetorical negation. Four infinitives define the true fast: (1) pattēaḥ ḥarṣubbôt-rèša' — to loosen the bonds of wickedness — the Pi'el pattēaḥ (open/loosen) applied to the fetters (ḥarṣubbôt) of oppression; (2) hatter 'ăguddôt môṭāh — to undo the straps of the yoke; (3) wĕšallēaḥ rĕṣuṣîm ḥopšîm — to let the oppressed go free — the Pual participle rĕṣuṣîm (crushed ones, from רָצַץ); (4) wĕk̠ol-môṭāh tĕnattēqû — to break every yoke.
7: hălō' pārōs lārā'ēb̠ laḥmĕkā — Is it not to break your bread with the hungry?. The verb pāras (פָּרַס — to break, divide) describes not giving leftovers but the active breaking of one's own bread. waʿăniyyîm mĕrûdîm tābî' bāyit — bring the homeless poor into your house — the adjective mĕrûdîm (wanderers, from רוּד — to roam) describes the rootless destitute. kî-tir'eh 'ārôm ûk̠issîtô — when you see the naked and you cover him — the conditional kî makes the encounter a test of the observer's character. The final clause: ûmibĕśārĕkā lō' tit'allām — and do not hide yourself from your own flesh.
8: 'āz yibbāqa' kaššaḥar 'ôrĕkā — then your light shall break forth like the dawn. The verb bāqa' (בָּקַע — to split, cleave, break open) is the word used for the Red Sea splitting (Exodus 14:21); applied to light, it is visceral rather than gradual — light forces its way through. ûrukātĕkā mĕhērāh tiṣmaḥ — your healing shall spring up speedily — the verb ṣāmaḥ (to sprout/grow) applied to healing; restoration is organic and rapid. wĕhālak̠ lĕpānêkā ṣidqĕkā — righteousness leads as a vanguard; k̠ĕbôd YHWH ya'asĕpĕkā — the glory of YHWH is the rear guard.
9: 'āz tiqrā' wĕYHWH ya'ăneh — then you will call and YHWH will answer. The prayer-response connection is restored by the ethical fast of Isaiah 58:6-7. Three practices that must cease: (1) môṣē' 'ĕṣba' — pointing the finger (accusation, contempt); (2) dibbĕr-'āwen — speaking wickedness/falsehood; (3) the broader cluster of oppressive behavior. The double Hiphil construction tāsēr mimmĕkā môṭāh — remove the yoke from your midst — the yoke of Isaiah 58:6 recurs as the condition for answered prayer.
10: wĕtāpēq lārā'ēb̠ napšĕkā — if you pour yourself out for the hungry. The verb pûq (Hiphil, tāpēq) means to bring forth, to pour out — the object is napšĕkā (your soul/self), the whole person offered as sustenance. wĕnèpeš na'ănāh taśbîa' — and satisfy the desire of the afflicted — the Pi'el taśbîa' (from שָׂבַע, to be satisfied) frames the action as truly meeting need, not token giving. Then: wĕzāraḥ baḥōšek̠ 'ôrĕkā — your light will rise in the darkness.
11: wĕnāḥăkā YHWH tāmîd — YHWH will guide you continually. The verb nāḥāh (Qal imperfect, to lead/guide) describes YHWH as the ongoing guide — not a one-time direction but continuous leading. wĕhiśbîa' bĕṣaḥṣāḥôt napšĕkā — and satisfy your desire in scorched places — ṣaḥṣāḥôt (parched, burning wastes) is the most inhospitable terrain; provision there is pure miracle. ûk̠môṣā' mayim 'ăšer lō'-yĕk̠azzĕb̠û mêmāyw — like a spring of water whose waters do not lie — the verb kāzab̠ (to lie/deceive) applied to springs that run dry; a spring that lies is one that promises water but fails. YHWH is the unfailing spring.
12: ûb̠ānû mimmĕkā ḥorbôt 'ôlām — your ancient ruins shall be rebuilt. The perfect b̠ānû used as future certain: those who come from you (your descendants) will rebuild what has been desolate for generations. The honorific titles: qārā' lĕkā gōdēr-pèreṣ — the repairer of the breach — the noun pèreṣ (breach, gap in a wall) is used in covenant contexts for the breaking down of divine protection; repairing it is covenant restoration. mĕšōbēb̠ nĕtîb̠ôt lāšèb̠et — the restorer of streets to dwell in.
13: 'im-tāšîb̠ mišabbāt raglĕkā — if you turn back your foot from the Sabbath. The idiom šûb̠ raglĕkā (restrain your foot) draws on walking-in-the-way imagery: the Sabbath is a day one does not travel on for one's own business. miṣṣ ōt ḥēp̄ĕṣĕkā bĕyôm qodšî — from doing your pleasure on my holy day — the noun ḥēpeṣ (pleasure/business) recurs from Isaiah 58:3 and Isaiah 58:2; the whole chapter is structured around who gets to pursue their ḥēpeṣ. wĕqārā'tā laššabbāt 'ōneg — and call the Sabbath a delight — 'ōneg (עֹנֶג, delight/luxury) shares the root with hit'annag (Isaiah 58:14); the Sabbath redesignated from burden to luxury.
14: 'āz tit'annaḡ 'al-YHWH — then you will delight yourself in YHWH. The Hithpa'el of עָנַג (to be delicate/luxurious — the same root as 'ōneg in Isaiah 58:13) is reflexive: taking delight for oneself in YHWH. The chapter closes with the promise: wĕhirk̠ab̠tîkā 'al-bāmôtê 'āreṣ — I will make you ride on the heights of the earth — the Hiphil of רָכַב̠ (to ride/mount); YHWH as the one who makes Israel mount to the heights. ûhăʾăk̠altîkā naḥălat ya'ăqōb̠ 'āb̠îkā — and feed you with the heritage of Jacob your father — covenant inheritance language closes the chapter: the true fast restores covenant standing. kî pî YHWH dibbēr — for the mouth of YHWH has spoken — the prophetic authentication formula.
